description: Learn how to add the final visual flourishes to make your charts professional and presentable.
text: In this lesson, you'll learn how to apply professional finishing touches to your data visualizations. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to enhance the readability and visual appeal of your charts through strategic use of themes, text formatting, and layout adjustments. You'll practice customizing titles, subtitles, axes, and other chart elements to create polished, publication-ready graphics. These skills will enable you to present your data journalism projects with a level of professionalism that meets industry standards.

description: Learn how to add the final visual flourishes to make your charts professional and presentable.
text: If you're a student of journalism, and you've taken your studies seriously, you've learned by now that often the most direct path to better writing is taking stuff out. Focus on the main message. The point. The "give a shit factor" an old editor of mine used to call it. Then ruthlessly remove everything that doesn't focus directly on that. A graphic is no different. "Just as a good editor of prose ruthlessly prunes out unnecessary words, so a designer of statistical graphics should prune out ink that fails to present fresh data-information," Tufte wrote in his 1983 work The Visual Display of Quantitative Information. "Although nothing can replace a good graphical idea applied to an interesting set of numbers, editing and revision are essential to sound graphical design work as they are to writing." Tufte's worldview on graphics can be described as minimalist. If it wasn't absolutely essential to the graphic, remove it. He wrote an entire chapter aiming his criticism at what he called Chartjunk -- unnecessary textures, flourishes, images and other distractions that took attention away from the data. He summarized his worldview thusly: > Above all else show the data. Maximize the data-ink ratio. Erase non-data-ink. Erase redundant data-ink. Revise and edit. At the time -- when computers were hard to come by and charts were ultimately printed to be consumed -- much of Tufte made sense. Anything extraneous to a chart cost money and time. But as time moved on, criticism of Tufte's militant minimalism started to grow. We find ourselves in a visual age. The internet is very visual. Our social media is largely built around images and video. For graphics to break through the visual noise, information designers argue now that a bit of art, a bit of creativity, is needed. You don't break Tufte's rules -- you bend them a little. The difference between a beginner and a professional graphic is a series of choices. They are the finishing touches that make all the differences. They'll be defined by what we include ... and what we remove.

description: Learn how to add the final visual flourishes to make your charts professional and presentable.
text: For the past two tutorials, we've been circling around the last bits you need to make a graphic into something publishable. With annotations, we learned about the need for additional text like headlines and chatter, as well as fixing the axis labels so an average person can read it. We learned that every graphic needs the following to be reasonably complete: * Headline * Chatter * Main body * Annotations * Labels * Source line * Credit line Here's a graphic from The New York Times, annotated: After text came color. We want to use color sparingly to draw attention. We want to use contrast to give people a giant arrow pointing at what we want them to see. The last step -- the finishing touches, if you will -- for a graphic boil down to two things: The first is ditching what ggplot does by default. The second is using typography to create a text hierarchy that separates our text and sets a level of importance in people's minds. Let's explore fixing up ggplot's output. We'll need a graphic to work with first, so I'm going to use the scatterplot we made in the annotations chapter. The first thing you should do with any chart that isn't a waffle chart -- more on that later -- is remove the default plot look and grey background by adding a pre-defined theme. There are two you should use to clean up your charts: `theme_minimal` or `theme_light`. These pre-packaged themes do just that. They make your chart cleaner and easier to read in one swoop. **NOTE**: Adding a `theme()` is something you do last. And if you're going to change theme elements, you need to **apply your prepackaged theme first**, then do your theme customization. Everything in ggplot is rendered in order. If you do custom theming, THEN apply the prepackaged theme, the prepackaged theme will overwrite anything you did. So `theme_minimal()` or `theme_light()` comes first.
code:
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(
    data=national, 
    aes(x=ADM_RATE, y=COSTT4_A),
    color="grey"
    ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=exclusivencheap, 
    aes(x=ADM_RATE, y=COSTT4_A),
    color="red"
    ) + 
  geom_text_repel(
    data=exclusivencheap, 
    aes(x=ADM_RATE, y=COSTT4_A, label=INSTNM),
    size=3
    ) + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.7246474) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 40745.59) +
  geom_text(aes(x=.87, y=85000, label="High admits, high cost"), color="blue") +
  geom_text(aes(x=.3, y=15000, label="Fewer admits, below average cost"), color="blue") + 
  labs(
    x="Admit rate", 
    y="Average annual cost", 
    title="Exclusive doesn't mean expensive", 
    subtitle="According to the federal Department of Education, there are seven schools who admit fewer than \n20 percent of applicants but are among the most affordable.", 
    caption="Source: US Department of Education | By Your Name"
    )

description: Learn how to add the final visual flourishes to make your charts professional and presentable.
text: Scroll down here and add `theme_minimal()` at the very end. We're just adding it on, like any other part of ggplot. Compare that to your previous chart. Easier to read, right? We're off to a good start, but our text has no real hierarchy. We want our headline to stand out more. So let's change that. When it comes to changing text, the place to do that is in the theme element. [There are a lot of ways to modify the theme](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/theme.html). An overwhelming number of ways to modify a theme. The dirty secret is we only need a few. The rest you look up when the need arises.

description: Learn how to add the final visual flourishes to make your charts professional and presentable.
text: Frequently in my classes, students use the waffle charts library quite extensively to make graphics. This is a quick walkthrough on how to get a waffle chart into a publication ready state. Let's make a quick waffle chart using my most favorite silly dataset: Climate prognostications from groundhogs, other assorted animals, taxidermied animals and other objects from around the United States. You thought Punxatawney Phil was the only one? Oh no. There were 73 others in 2024 who made a prediction on early spring vs. longer winter. So what was the consensus? Let's visualize. We can add labels and themes, but you have to be careful. For this to look like a waffle chart, we have to use `theme_void()`. Here is an example of how to use ggplot's `labs` and the `theme` to make a fully publication ready graphic. They just get added with a + after the waffle. The same additions work for the multiple chart version from the waffle chapter. Note: These finishing touches aren't optional. They're required of every graphic you create going forward. They are what makes professional graphics look professional. It's the careful consideration of every element on the screen and how they impact a reader's understanding of the graphic.
code:
ggplot() +
  geom_tile(
    data=waffle_predictions, 
    aes(
      x = x, y = y, fill = category
      ), 
    color = "white", 
    linewidth = .5) +
  coord_equal() + 
  theme_void() + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("yellow", "lightblue")) +
  labs(
    x = "1 square = 1 prediction",
    title="Early spring it is", 
    subtitle="The groundhogs, lobsters and other assorted predictors from around the \nUS and Canada were decisvely feeling spring this year.",
    caption="Source: Groundhog-Day.com | Graphic by Matt Waite") + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title.y = element_blank()
  )

text: - Theme: A predefined set of visual parameters in ggplot2 that control the overall appearance of a chart. - `element_text()`: A function used within `theme()` to modify text properties like size, color, and font face. - `element_blank()`: A function used to remove chart elements entirely, such as gridlines. - `plot.title`: The theme element that controls the appearance of the chart's main title. - `plot.subtitle`: The theme element that controls the appearance of the chart's subtitle. - `axis.title`: The theme element that controls the appearance of axis labels. - `panel.grid`: The theme element that controls the appearance of gridlines in the chart. - `plot.title.position`: A theme setting that adjusts the alignment of the chart title. - `labs()`: A function in ggplot2 used to add or modify chart labels, including title, subtitle, and axis labels.

tutorial: Data Journalism Lesson 27: Datawrapper for charts
description: Making quick graphics for publication
text: In this lesson, you'll learn how to use Datawrapper, a powerful online tool for creating interactive charts and maps. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to upload data, choose appropriate chart types, customize visual elements, and add annotations to your visualizations. You'll practice these skills by creating a scatterplot of colleges, gaining hands-on experience in transforming raw data into a publication ready graphic. This ability to quickly create professional visualizations will be invaluable for presenting your data findings in a clear and engaging manner.

tutorial: Data Journalism Lesson 27: Datawrapper for charts
description: Making quick graphics for publication
text: It's rare that you can get 1,000 data journalists to stare, mouths hanging open, in awe, but Gregor Aisch did it in under 5 minutes. In 2015, in lightning talks at the NICAR conference in Atlanta, Aisch demoed a project he had been building inside The New York Times called Mr. Chartmaker. It was a browser-based tool that allowed you to upload data, pick a chart type, change some text and publish a NYT-level graphic in minutes. Today, that seems like no big deal. Indeed, there were already online charting tools around that time. The problem? They were trying to be everything to everyone, and in the process, they could only make simple charts in very bland ways. Aisch manange to make **New York Times** level graphics with his online chart maker. Most of us in the room that day for the demo felt like we were watching the gods give fire to the mortals. It was a revelation. And we were all jealous as hell that this tool only existed inside the Times. Lucky for us, Aisch co-founded a company called Datawrapper. Technically, he started it before he went to work for the Times, and came back to it after he left. Today, Datawrapper does everything that demo showed us back in the day and more. And anyone can get an account for free. Making charts in Datawrapper is preposterously simple, which is the point. There are dozens of chart types, and dozens of options. To get from a csv to a chart to publication is very, very easy. So you might be asking right now ... if it's so easy, why did we just go through all that stuff using R and `ggplot` to make graphics? Well, young scholar, the *reason* Datawrapper is so easy is *because* you just went through all that work to get to where we are now. Because you made graphics with your bare hands, you now understand the pieces that go into it. The thinking. The reason certain things are as they are. What charts require. All of the choices in Datawrapper -- once you get past some differences in what things are called and figure out what is where -- will make complete sense to you. And, having both tools at your disposal means knowing when one works and one doesn't. Datawrapper works *great* for simple charts, especially ones that have to get done quick and put online to match the pace of news. But hopefully, the gods of journalism will smile upon you and bless you with a story and some ambition that goes beyond what Datawrapper can handle. That's when knowing how to write code comes in handy. But enough of this. Let's dive in.

tutorial: Data Journalism Lesson 27: Datawrapper for charts
description: Making quick graphics for publication
text: First, go to [datawrapper.de](https://www.datawrapper.de/) and sign up for an account. It’s free. Next, we need some data. Datawrapper has multiple ways to pull in data. You quite literally can copy and paste from a spreadsheet into it. You can share the url to a Google Sheet with it. You can upload your own csv made from your own data. To do that, it's just `write_csv(dataframename, "filename.csv")` and you'll have a csv file, called filename.csv in your directory where your notebook is. But we don't even have to do that. We can feed it a url of a csv straight away. So copy this url: `https://the-art-of-data-journalism.github.io/tutorial-data/college-cost/national.csv` In your Datawrapper dashboard, we want to start with a new chart. So let's make a chart. We're going to make a chart, similar to what we did in the Finishing Touches chapter. When you pick chart, it's going to take you right to the steps of making a chart. The first thing we do is add our csv file to Datawrapper. Click Link external data and paste your url into the spot where it says enter url. Everything should work fine and you can hit Proceed. Datawrapper is now going to show you what it thinks your data is like. Text will appear in black, numbers in blue, dates in green. If there's something in red, that means there's a problem. Your data is very simple, so very little to go wrong. You can hit proceed. Once you do, it's going to show you all of the chart types you can make, and it's going to guess (wrongly) at one. Pick a scatterplot and hit proceed. In the next window, we're going to do two things: First, select the correct columns for the correct axes. Set the horizontal axis column to ADM_RATE. Then, change the vertical axis to COSTT4_A. Click proceed. Now we're on the annotate screen. A lot of work is going to be done here. This is where we add all important text to our chart. What are required parts? Headline, chatter, annotations, credit and source lines, right? Well, here they're title, description, data source and byline. Another incredibly important piece here: alternative text for screen readers. There are millions of visually impaired people on the internet every day who experience visual parts of the internet through alt-text. Be a good human and add some. So we can borrow from previous work here. For a title, try: Not all exclusive schools are expensive. For a description: According to federal data, there are seven schools who admit fewer than 20 percent of applicants but are among the more affordable colleges in the US. The data source is the US Department of Education's College Scorecard. The url for it is https://collegescorecard.ed.gov/ As a challenge, try to describe the scatterplot you are making as if you were describing it to someone who can't see in the alternative text box. Scroll down below the text boxes we've been filling out. What makes Datawrapper great is that it's interactive, which means people can click on the dots and get the data back. But, we need to enable that and we need to fix some stuff. First, you can make the Label column the INSTNM. Then, turn on Show tooltips. A full explanation of all you can do here is beyond the scope of this tutorial -- there's a lot -- but Datawrapper has its own templating language in the tooltips that can help you make the pop-up box into something human readable. Click in the top box and then click on the INSTNM box below the tooltips. Now the headline for your pop up box is the institution name. If you want to verify, just hover over a dot in your chart. Then, in the main text box, try this: ``` Average annual cost: ${{ costt4_a }}</br> Admit rate: {{ adm_rate }} ``` Where you get to the {{ cost4_a }} bits -- I didn't have to type that. You can click the blue rectangles below and it will fill them in. If you click on the down-arrow next to the column names, you'll see a bunch of formatting options. Hang onto that for a moment -- we're going to fix that. Normally, we could proceed to the publish screen but we've got some warts. For example -- the x-axis and y-axis labels aren't very reader friendly. And our admit rate on the bottom isn't a percentage -- it's a decimal still. There aren't universities admitting just .2 percent of applicants. That should be 20 percent. But how do we fix all of that? The easiest way is to go back a few steps. Click on the Check and Describe step up near the top of your chart. Here, we can do several transformations at once, just like this is a spreadsheet. For example: Change your ADM_RATE column name to Admit rate. For the COSTT4_A column, click in there and make it Average annual cost. Then, if you click on the D at the top of the spreadsheet, it will highlight the column but it will also open up new tools on the left. Note one is Divide/multiply by and it defaults to (no change). If you click that, you'll see there's a Multiply by 100 option. That's right -- this is so common that they just made a click to fix. That will get us 99.9 percent of the way there. Want to be extremely nit-picky? Got that editor urge to make everything absolutely perfect? Click back on the Visualize step, and under the Horizontal axis area, change the Number format from (automatic) to 0% to get that sweet sweet percent sign on the bottom axis labels. Completely necessary? No. A sign of professional attention to detail? Yes. You can hit proceed until you get to the Publish and Embed step. That is the last screen. On this screen, there's a big Publish Now button. If you want to embed your interactive chart onto a website, you'll have to click that. Then, follow the instructions on your content management system for embedding media. One thing I'll point out is that you can also download your chart as a static image. Publishing to Instagram or Blue Sky? Need a static image to be the display image in your CMS? This is how you can get your chart out in a clean format easily.

description: Making quick graphics for publication
text: In this lesson, you'll learn how to create interactive maps using Datawrapper, a powerful online visualization tool. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to prepare data for mapping, create symbol maps with customized colors and tooltips, and add essential elements like titles and source information. You'll practice these skills using real groundhog prediction data, gaining hands-on experience in transforming raw data into an engaging, interactive visualization. This ability to create professional-looking maps will be invaluable for presenting geographic data in your journalism projects.

tutorial: Data Journalism Lesson 28: Datawrapper for maps
description: Making quick graphics for publication
text: Past students in my data journalism class have heard me say this before: if I could make my 16-week class a 32-week class, the first 16 weeks would stay the same. They would remain the same chapters you've worked through here. The remaining 16 weeks would be all about maps. Maps are a truly incredible data journalism tool. They are a universal language that nearly all of us speak. They are built for things like context, but also allow us to indulge in a little selfishness. What do you do every time you see a weather map? Find your neighborhood right? What do you do when you see a county map shaded by anything? Find yours, no? Here's a map of every university in the country and some piece of data about it that you really want to know. The first dot you're clicking on is your university, guaranteed. The problem is that maps are hard. If you're a university student reading this right now, somewhere on your campus are whole sections of multiple courses at multiple levels of the curriculum that are all about using Geographic Information Systems to do analysis. Geographic analysis has all of the problems of data analysis -- incomplete, incorrect, poorly formatted data, for example -- and adds to it the math of representing physical space. What types of problems are those? Well, consider this: The Earth is not round. No, this isn't Flat Earth Propaganda making its way into this textbook. The Earth is a spheroid, and not only that, it's an oblate spheroid, which is a funny way of saying it's squished from the top, fattening out the middle. Now, take things on that oblate spheroid and take them from a three-dimensional shape and flatten them into a two-dimensional shape. Map nerds can go on all day about how the distortions that occur from that flattening screws up our understanding of the world. What I want you to understand is this: There are so many ways that people have come up with to solve this problem that counting them is impossible. There is no comprehensive list of map projections in the world *because the set of possible outcomes is infinite*. All that is to say that doing sophisticated analysis with maps and spatial data is hard. Enter Datawrapper.

tutorial: Data Journalism Lesson 28: Datawrapper for maps
description: Making quick graphics for publication
text: Making charts in Datawrapper is preposterously simple. Making maps is less so, but not hard. It involves you choosing a base map and making sure your data has geographic elements to them. Those might be FIPS numbers to match to counties. Those might be latitude and longitude coordinate points. There are others, but those are two of the biggest ones. First, we need some data. And for this one, we're going to have some fun. Throughout this course, you've dealt with mostly serious data. Plane crashes and tax-payer funded grants and college tuition bills. Let's return to the data we used for a waffle chart in Chapter 26: [Groundhog-Day.com](https://groundhog-day.com/). Groundhog Day, the day when a rodent in Pennslyvania comes out of a hole and if it sees its shadow, we're all miserable for a while longer because we're all sick of winter. Remember I told you that Punxatawny Phil is not the only weather prognosticating rodent in the United States? Not even close. There are 74 ... things ... that people consult about the the coming forecast. Why do I say things? Because the things people consult are ... eclectic. Sure there are lots of groundhogs -- 32 of them in 2024. But did you know that seven other communities in the United States and Canada consult a *DEAD* groundhog? Or, as Groundhog-Day.com politely describes them, taxidermied groundhogs. Six more ask a *person in a groundhog suit* if winter will end early. Then there are the places who don't use groundhogs, and instead use a chicken, or an alligator, or a lobster, beaver, bullfrog, prairie dog or ... a statue of a prairie dog. So like every presidential election, we have poll aggregators who average together poll results to try and predict a winner. You now are a Groundhog aggregator to find out the truth about if winter will end early or not. I've gone ahead to Groundhog-Day.com and scraped the data. You need this url: ``` https://the-art-of-data-journalism.github.io/tutorial-data/groundhogs/predictions2024.csv ``` In your Datawrapper dashboard, we want to start with a new chart. One thing Datawrapper does exceptionally well is create interactive maps. So let's make one. We're going to make a symbol map because our data is made up of points. Another common map type is the Choropleth map where each unit of geography is shaded in by the value. Our data includes points in Canada, so we want to select North America as our base map and then proceed. Here's where that work of creating the data comes in. Similar to the last exercise, click the Link external data button below the Upload area. There you can paste in the url of your data into the box and then click Connect. We'll go to match and make sure Datawrapper found our latitude and longitude coordinates and that it all worked. While there, we're also going to select the column for the symbol color. That's the last box on the Match screen. Set it to shadow. Click proceed twice from there to get to the Refine screen. We'll start to work with color palettes. You should change it to Steps given that we're going to shade it by the shadow column -- which is only 1 or 0. Now we can fiddle with the legend to make it so instead of saying 1 or 0 it says long winter or early spring. Now we need to annotate our chart. We need a headline, some chatter (the description), the [data source](https://groundhog-day.com/) and a credit line. One great part of interactive maps are tooltips, where you can click on the dot and get information about it. So let's enable tooltips and customize them. You can always test what you see on the tooltips using the preview map on the right. Hover over a dot, see what it looks like. If you're happy with it, go on and proceed to the end. Similar to charts, you can publish the interactive version on your website, or you can save a static image out for other platforms that can't handle HTML embeds.

description: Making quick graphics for publication
text: Throughout this lesson, you've mastered the process of creating interactive maps with Datawrapper. You've learned to prepare your data in R, import it into Datawrapper, and customize various aspects of your map, including color schemes, legends, and tooltips. Remember, effective data visualization is about more than just plotting points -- it's about telling a story through your data. Consider how tools like Datawrapper can help you present complex geographic information in a clear, engaging format that resonates with your audience.

tutorial: Data Journalism Lesson 28: Datawrapper for maps
description: Making quick graphics for publication
text: - Symbol map: A type of map that uses symbols or markers to represent data points at specific geographic locations. - Choropleth map: A thematic map where areas are shaded or patterned proportionally to a statistical variable. - Base map: The underlying map onto which data is plotted, often showing geographic features like country borders or continents. - Latitude: The angular distance of a place north or south of the earth's equator, expressed in degrees. - Longitude: The angular distance of a place east or west of the Prime Meridian in Greenwich, London, expressed in degrees. - Tooltip: An informational box that appears when hovering over or clicking on a data point in an interactive visualization. - Color palette: A set of colors chosen to represent different categories or values in a visualization. - Legend: An explanatory list of the symbols or colors used on a map or chart. - Chatter: In journalism, a brief explanatory text that follows the headline and provides context for the visualization. - Interactive map: A digital map that allows users to interact with the data, often through clicking, hovering, or zooming.
